passages.explanation: King Herod Agrippa I begins persecuting the church. He has James — the brother of John, one of Jesus' inner three — killed with the sword. When he sees this pleases the Jewish leaders, he arrests Peter during the Festival of Unleavened Bread, intending to put him on public trial after Passover. Peter is placed under heavy guard: four squads of four soldiers each. But the church is earnestly praying for him. Herod has political motives; the church has prayer. The contrast sets up what follows.

The night before his trial, Peter is sleeping between two soldiers, bound with chains, with guards at the door. An angel appears, light fills the cell, and the angel strikes Peter on the side to wake him: quick, get up. The chains fall off his wrists. The angel tells him to put on his sandals and cloak and follow. Peter obeys but thinks he's seeing a vision — he doesn't realise it's actually happening. They pass the first guard, the second guard, and the iron gate to the city, which opens by itself. They walk the length of one street, and the angel disappears. Peter comes to himself and says: now I know for certain that the Lord has sent his angel and rescued me from Herod's grasp.

Peter goes to the house of Mary, the mother of John Mark, where many are gathered praying. He knocks at the outer gate, and a servant named Rhoda comes to answer. She recognises Peter's voice and is so overjoyed she runs back without opening the gate, announcing that Peter is standing outside. They tell her she's out of her mind. She insists. They say it must be his angel. Meanwhile Peter keeps knocking. When they finally open the door and see him, they're astonished. Peter motions for them to be quiet, explains how the Lord brought him out of prison, and tells them to report this to James (Jesus' brother, now leading the Jerusalem church) and the other believers. Then he leaves for another place. The scene is almost comic — they're praying for his release but can't believe it when he shows up.

At daylight there's no small commotion among the soldiers over what happened to Peter. Herod searches for him, can't find him, cross-examines the guards, and orders them executed. Roman military law held guards responsible with their own lives for escaped prisoners. Herod then leaves Judea and goes down to Caesarea. Peter's escape has real consequences for others.

Herod is in a dispute with the people of Tyre and Sidon, who depend on his territory for food. They secure an audience through Blastus, Herod's personal attendant, and seek peace. On the appointed day, Herod puts on his royal robes, sits on his throne, and delivers a speech. The crowd shouts: this is the voice of a god, not a man! Herod accepts the worship — and immediately an angel of the Lord strikes him down, because he didn't give glory to God. He's eaten by worms and dies. The Jewish historian Josephus records a similar account. The contrast is stark: the angel who freed Peter in the night now strikes down the king who imprisoned him.

The word of God continues to spread and flourish — Luke's characteristic summary statement. Barnabas and Saul finish their mission in Jerusalem and return to Antioch, taking John Mark with them. The persecution hasn't stopped the church; it's fuelled it. Herod is dead, Peter is free, and the gospel keeps moving.
